Journal.Title: The New England journal of medicine

Article.ArticleTitle: Ventilation with lower tidal volumes as compared with traditional tidal volumes for acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome.

Abstract.AbstractText: Traditional approaches to mechanical ventilation use tidal volumes of 10 to 15 ml per kilogram of body weight and may cause stretch-induced lung injury in patients with acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome. We conducted a multicenter, randomized trial comparing traditional tidal volumes with lower tidal volumes.

Patients with acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome were enrolled in a randomized trial. The trial compared traditional ventilation with an initial tidal volume of 12 ml per kilogram of predicted body weight and a plateau pressure of 50 cm of water or less, with ventilation with a lower tidal volume of 6 ml per kilogram of predicted body weight and a plateau pressure of 30 cm of water or less. The primary outcomes were death before a patient was discharged home and breathing without assistance and the number of days without ventilator use during the first 28 days. n = 861.

The trial was stopped after the enrollment of 861 patients because mortality was lower in the group treated with lower tidal volumes than in the group treated with traditional tidal volumes (31.0 percent vs. 39.8 percent, P = 0.007).

In patients with acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome, mechanical ventilation with a lower tidal volume than is traditionally used results in decreased mortality and increases the number of days without ventilator use.

Author.CollectiveName: The Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome Network
